steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for a generalization specifically about rails-with-trails — trails that share a right-of-way with an active rail line. A generalization must make a broader claim about this category using evidence from the notes, not just restate a specific fact.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'In the US, trails have been constructed from disused corridors as well as on property parallel to active rail lines.': This describes both trail types together without making any generalization specifically about rails-with-trails. ✗
  • Choice B — 'In the US, trails that share a right-of-way with an active rail line are less common than some other types of railway trails.': This draws a generalization directly about rails-with-trails using the numerical contrast in the notes: approximately 400 rails-with-trails vs. more than 2,000 rail trails. ✓
  • Choice C — 'The Union Pacific Railroad Trail is one of approximately 400 US trails that share a right-of-way with an active rail line.': This restates a specific fact from the notes rather than making a broader generalization about the rails-with-trails category. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Since the 1960s, thousands of public trails in the US have been created from disused railroad corridors.': This is a specific fact about rail trails (disused corridors), not a generalization about rails-with-trails. It addresses the wrong category. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
